name: Stewart Mine
short_description: BLM rockhounding site near Pala known for tourmaline, feldspar, and quartz specimens. Personal use collecting allowed with standard BLM limits.
description:
Stewart Mine is a public BLM rockhounding location in San Diego County's famous Pala gem district, one of California's premier tourmaline-producing regions. The site sits at 1018 feet elevation on moderate slopes characteristic of the Peninsular Ranges. This area has a rich history of gemstone discovery, particularly tourmaline and feldspar, which formed during Mesozoic pegmatitic intrusions that created ideal conditions for crystal growth.

The geology here reflects classic California pegmatite formation - granite and granitic pegmatites rich in rare earth minerals and colorful gem varieties. Tourmaline is the primary draw, with specimens ranging from gem-quality to specimen-grade pieces. Feldspar, quartz, and beryl also occur throughout the area. The moderate slopes and accessible terrain make this suitable for families and beginners, though experienced collectors will find rewarding material as well.

Collecting at Stewart Mine is straightforward for personal use rockhounders. The BLM designation means no permits are required, and the standard California BLM rules apply: 25 pounds per day maximum, personal use only, and no motorized excavation. The site sees moderate visitation, particularly on weekends, so arriving early increases your chances of quality finds. Respect existing claim markers and other collectors' work areas.
history: The Pala district has been a significant tourmaline and feldspar source since the late 1800s, with both artisanal and commercial mining operations developing along pegmatite veins. Stewart Mine and surrounding claims developed during the gem boom era when San Diego County tourmalines gained international recognition. The pegmatites here formed during Mesozoic granitic intrusions 70-100 million years ago, with hydrothermal fluids creating the tourmaline, feldspar, and quartz crystals that make this region world-famous.
tips: Search along exposed pegmatite outcrops and in the weathered soil around the mine workings where crystals weather out naturally. Tourmaline specimens often show as dark striations or small prismatic crystals on feldspar or quartz faces - look along hillside exposures and in erosion gullies. Bring a small hand pick or chisel to carefully work specimens free from host rock, and screen finer material at your feet for smaller gem pieces. The moderate slope can produce decent exposure of fresh material after winter rains, so check the site after wet weather.
terrain_notes: Elevation 1018 feet with moderate slopes typical of the Peninsular Ranges foothills. Terrain is hilly with exposed pegmatite outcrops, scattered boulders, and rocky ground. Vegetation includes chaparral scrub and scattered oak. Walking distance from parking is typically less than one-half mile to productive collecting areas. Footing requires sturdy boots due to loose rocks and uneven terrain. No shade available - exposure is full sun.
written_directions: From Pala, head north on Highway 76 toward Pauma Valley. Continue approximately 3 miles north and watch for BLM signage indicating public rockhounding areas. Turn east on the marked dirt access road. Drive approximately 1-2 miles to the main parking area near Stewart Mine. The road is passable by passenger vehicle under normal conditions but becomes rough after heavy rain.
road_conditions: Unpaved dirt road, passable by passenger vehicle but rough after rain. No major washboard under normal conditions. High-clearance preferred.
parking_notes: Informal pullout area near Stewart Mine workings. Space for 5-8 vehicles. No facilities. Parking is free.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA. No commercial collection. No motorized excavation. Respect existing claim markers. Pack out all trash. No surface disturbance beyond hand tools.

nearest_services: Pala has basic services 3-4 miles south. Larger towns including Fallbrook (8 miles) and Oceanside (20 miles) offer full services including gas, food, and water.
accessibility_notes: Moderate terrain with uneven rocky slopes and loose material. Not wheelchair accessible. Sturdy hiking boots required. No maintained trails. Moderate physical fitness recommended. Shade is minimal and heat exposure significant in summer.

faq.question: What tourmaline and gemstones can you find at Stewart Mine in California?
faq.answer: Stewart Mine is located in the famous Pala tourmaline district of San Diego County. You can find tourmaline (often gem-quality), feldspar, quartz, beryl, and mica. Tourmaline is the primary attraction, with specimens ranging from small crystals to larger specimen-grade pieces. Feldspar often hosts tourmaline and is collectible in its own right.

faq.question: Do you need a permit to rockhound at Stewart Mine near Pala?
faq.answer: No permit is required at Stewart Mine - it is public BLM land open to recreational rockhounding under California's FLPMA guidelines. You can collect year-round for personal use with no paperwork or fees. The only requirement is that you follow the 25 pound per day limit and respect other collectors and claim markers.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to visit Stewart Mine for rockhounding?
faq.answer: October through April is ideal for collecting at Stewart Mine. The cooler temperatures and lower intense sun make for more comfortable hiking on the exposed hillsides. Winter rains also help weather out fresh material. Summer can be extremely hot with full sun exposure and little shade on the moderate slopes.

faq.question: Is Stewart Mine beginner-friendly for family rockhounding trips?
faq.answer: Yes, Stewart Mine is excellent for beginners and families. The terrain is relatively accessible with moderate slopes and short walks to productive areas. No special skills or permits are required - just bring basic hand tools like a small pick or chisel. Kids ages 8 and up typically do well here with adult supervision. The informal atmosphere and abundance of collectible material make it rewarding for first-time rockhounders.

faq.question: What tools and equipment do you need to rockhound at Stewart Mine?
faq.answer: Bring a small hand pick, chisel, or flatbar for carefully extracting specimens from pegmatite outcrops. A backpack with water is essential - there are no facilities and the sun exposure is intense. Wear sturdy boots for the rocky, uneven terrain and consider a small brush for cleaning specimens. A bucket or bag helps carry finds up to your 25 pound daily limit. Sun protection (hat, sunscreen) is critical.

faq.question: How far is Stewart Mine from San Diego and what are the road conditions?
faq.answer: Stewart Mine is approximately 30-35 miles north of downtown San Diego, near the town of Pala. Access is via Highway 76 north from the coast or from the I-15 corridor. The final approach is a dirt road passable by standard passenger vehicles under normal conditions, though it becomes rough after heavy rain. High-clearance vehicles are preferred. The drive from central San Diego takes about 45 minutes to one hour.
